name: Riggs, Sara Lu
recent_1: El Iskandarani, M., Zhang, P., & Riggs, S. L. (2026). Multitasking Across Physical and Virtual Displays: The Effect of Spatial Discontinuity and Task Load. IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics, 32(5). DOI: 10.1109/TVCG.2026.3679118
recent_1_synopsis: This study examines how spatially discontinuous information spread across physical and virtual (head-mounted) displays affects multitasking performance under varying task load. Using a mixed-reality setup, the authors quantify the costs of switching attention across physical-virtual display boundaries. The work informs the design of cross-reality workspaces where operators must integrate information from co-located physical screens and AR/VR overlays.
recent_2: El Iskandarani, M., Bolton, M. L., & Riggs, S. L. (2026). Multitasking Tug-of-War: Exploring the Impact of Task Modality, Task Load Level, and Task Load Type on Dual-Task Interference in Virtual Reality. Human Factors, 68(4). DOI: 10.1177/00187208251396766
recent_2_synopsis: Using a dual-task paradigm in virtual reality, this paper disentangles how task modality (visual vs. auditory), task load level, and task load type jointly drive dual-task interference. The findings extend multiple-resource theory into immersive VR environments, showing which combinations of concurrent demands produce the steepest performance costs. Results offer guidance for allocating tasks across sensory channels in VR-based work and training.
recent_3: El Iskandarani, M., Bolton, M. L., & Riggs, S. L. (2025). Examining dual-task interference effects of visual and auditory perceptual load in virtual reality. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 205, 103619. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2025.103619
recent_3_synopsis: This experiment manipulates visual and auditory perceptual load independently to characterize how each contributes to dual-task interference in VR. It tests whether perceptual-load theory, originally developed for 2D laboratory tasks, generalizes to immersive multitasking. The study clarifies when high perceptual load in one modality spares versus impairs concurrent processing in another, with implications for multimodal VR interface design.
seminal_1: Lu, S. A., Wickens, C. D., Prinet, J. C., Hutchins, S. D., Sarter, N., & Sebok, A. (2013). Supporting Interruption Management and Multimodal Interface Design: Three Meta-Analyses of Task Performance as a Function of Interrupting Task Modality. Human Factors, 55(4), 697-724. DOI: 10.1177/0018720813476298
seminal_1_synopsis: This is Riggs' (published under her earlier name S. A. Lu) most-cited work, with approximately 144 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026). Through three meta-analyses, it synthesizes how the modality of an interrupting task (visual, auditory, tactile) shapes performance on both the interrupting and ongoing tasks, grounding multimodal interface design in quantitative evidence. It is foundational for interruption-management research and for applying multiple-resource theory to multimodal display design.
seminal_2: Clark, L. D., Bhagat, A. B., & Riggs, S. L. (2020). Extending Fitts' law in three-dimensional virtual environments with current low-cost virtual reality technology. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 139, 102413. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2020.102413
seminal_2_synopsis: With approximately 139 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), this is among Riggs' most influential papers and a widely cited reference for VR interaction modeling. It empirically tests and extends Fitts' law to 3D discrete pointing tasks using commodity head-mounted VR hardware, showing how target size, distance, and inclination angle affect movement time. The work is foundational for designing and benchmarking selection/pointing interactions in low-cost virtual reality.
expertise_overview: Sara Lu Riggs studies human attention and performance in complex, multitasking-intensive work environments such as aviation, healthcare/anesthesia monitoring, military UAV command-and-control, and manufacturing. Her central interests are attention management, interruption management, and how operators divide cognitive resources across concurrent tasks and information sources. A substantial thread of her work concerns multimodal and multisensory displays, especially tactile and crossmodal (visual-tactile, auditory-tactile) cueing, including tactile change blindness/detection and crossmodal stimulus-intensity matching methods. More recently she investigates multitasking, visual search, and dual-task interference in virtual and augmented reality, as well as human-AI interaction topics such as reliance on explainable AI and trust in AI code generation. She also applies eye-tracking and computational methods (e.g., cross-recurrence quantification, machine learning) to measure workload and team coordination. Her methodological contributions include meta-analytic synthesis of multimodal interruption effects and extending Fitts' law to 3D VR.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. Confirmed as Sara Lu Riggs, Associate Professor, Dept. of Engineering Systems and Environment (Systems and Information Engineering), University of Virginia; PhD Industrial & Operations Engineering, University of Michigan (2014), advisor Nadine Sarter. Earlier publications appear under 'S. A. Lu' / 'Sara A. Lu' (maiden name) and 'Sara Riggs'/'S. L. Riggs'; the 2013 seminal meta-analysis is authored by S. A. Lu with co-author N. Sarter, consistent with this identity. Sources: UVA faculty page (engineering.virginia.edu/faculty/sara-lu-riggs), Google Scholar (user=ALjgMAoAAAAJ), ResearchGate, dblp (pid 178/3520). Citation counts (total ~1,009; h-index 16) and per-paper counts (seminal_1 ~144, seminal_2 ~139) from Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17; treat as approximate. All five cited DOIs were verified via Crossref API (HTTP 200, matching title/authors/year) on 2026-06-17: 10.1109/TVCG.2026.3679118, 10.1177/00187208251396766, 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2025.103619, 10.1177/0018720813476298, 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2020.102413. No unverifiable citations included. Note: recent_2 'Multitasking Tug-of-War' carries a 2025 online-first date (DOI verified) and a 2026 Human Factors print volume; both recent_1 and recent_2 are dated 2026.
